question: Does enabling HTTP/2 multiplexing on the API gateway reduce p99 latency by at least 20% compared to HTTP/1.1 under 500 concurrent connections?
nullHypothesis: Enabling HTTP/2 multiplexing has no significant effect on p99 latency; mean p99 under HTTP/2 equals mean p99 under HTTP/1.1
alternativeHypothesis: Enabling HTTP/2 multiplexing reduces p99 latency by at least 20% compared to HTTP/1.1 under 500 concurrent connections
controlVariables: Synthetic load profile (500 concurrent connections, 1,000 req/sec)
Payload size (fixed at 2 KB JSON response)
procedure: Deploy two identical API gateway instances: one configured for HTTP/1.1, one for HTTP/2
Run synthetic load test using k6 at 500 concurrent connections for 10 minutes on each instance
Record p50, p95, and p99 latency distributions and error rates for each run
Repeat each run 3 times and average results to reduce variance
Apply Welch's t-test to compare p99 distributions with α = 0.05
successCriteria: HTTP/2 p99 latency is ≥20% lower than HTTP/1.1 p99, with statistical significance (p < 0.05) and error rate remaining below 0.1%
result: HTTP/1.1 p99: 342 ms (avg over 3 runs). HTTP/2 p99: 201 ms (avg over 3 runs). Error rate: 0.02% on both. Welch's t-test: p = 0.003.
conclusion: Reject H₀. HTTP/2 reduces p99 latency by 41% (342 ms → 201 ms), exceeding the 20% threshold with statistical significance (p = 0.003). Enable HTTP/2 on the production API gateway.
limitations: Synthetic load test does not capture real-world request size variability or user session patterns
Experiment was run in the same VPC — cross-region or CDN edge effects are not captured
HTTP/2 server push was disabled; enabling it may yield further improvements or introduce overhead
